modules.title: What AI Is (and Isn't)
objectives: Define artificial intelligence in plain language
Distinguish AI from ordinary software and from science-fiction myths
Recognise everyday systems that already use AI
keyPoints: AI is software that performs tasks we associate with human intelligence, such as recognising patterns, predicting, and generating language.
Most real-world AI is narrow: it does one kind of task well, not everything.
AI learns from data and probabilities rather than following only fixed, hand-written rules.
AI does not understand or feel; it produces statistically likely outputs that can look intelligent.
modules.example: Your email spam filter is AI: it learned from millions of labelled emails which patterns signal spam, instead of a person writing a rule for every possible spam message.
modules.summary: AI is pattern-based software that mimics aspects of human intelligence. Today's AI is narrow and capable, but it is not conscious or all-knowing.

modules.title: How Machine Learning Learns
objectives: Explain how a model learns from examples (training)
Describe the role of data quality
Understand why models can be wrong or biased
keyPoints: Machine learning finds patterns in training data and uses them to make predictions on new data.
Garbage in, garbage out: biased or incomplete data produces biased or unreliable models.
Models are evaluated on accuracy, but high accuracy does not guarantee fairness or safety.
A model generalises from the past, so it can fail when the world changes (data drift).
modules.example: A model trained only on photos taken in daylight may perform poorly at night, because its training data never represented that situation.
modules.summary: Machine learning generalises patterns from data. The quality and representativeness of that data largely determines how trustworthy the model is.

modules.title: Generative AI & Large Language Models
objectives: Explain at a high level how an LLM produces text
Define tokens, context window, and hallucination
keyPoints: Generative AI creates new content (text, images, audio, code) rather than only classifying or predicting.
A Large Language Model (LLM) predicts the next token (a piece of a word) over and over to produce fluent text.
The context window is how much text the model can consider at once; older text outside it is forgotten.
Hallucination is when a model produces confident but false information; outputs must be verified.
modules.example: Ask an LLM for a citation and it may invent a real-looking but non-existent source. The text is plausible because the model optimises for likely wording, not truth.
modules.summary: Generative AI and LLMs produce fluent new content by predicting likely tokens. They are powerful but can hallucinate, so human verification is essential.

modules.title: Using AI Tools Effectively
objectives: Write clearer instructions to AI tools
Choose the right tool for a task
Keep a human in the loop
keyPoints: Give context, a clear task, and the desired format to get better results.
Treat AI as a fast junior assistant: helpful for drafts, summaries, and ideas, but check its work.
Break big requests into steps; review and refine outputs iteratively.
Never paste confidential or personal data into tools that are not approved for it.
modules.example: Instead of 'write about marketing', try 'write a 150-word LinkedIn post for small-business owners about email marketing, friendly tone, one call to action'.
modules.summary: You get the most from AI by giving clear context and format, working iteratively, and always reviewing outputs before relying on them.

modules.title: Responsible & Ethical AI
objectives: Know when to escalate or seek oversight
keyPoints: Key risks: bias, privacy violations, misinformation, over-reliance, and lack of transparency.
Protect privacy by minimising and anonymising data; follow your organisation's policies.
Be transparent that AI was used where it matters, and keep accountability with a person.
High-impact decisions (hiring, lending, health) need extra scrutiny and human oversight.
modules.example: An AI resume screener that learned from past hiring may favour one group; auditing for bias and keeping a human reviewer helps prevent unfair outcomes.
modules.summary: Responsible AI means actively managing bias, privacy, and accuracy risks, being transparent, and keeping humans accountable for important decisions.

modules.title: AI at Work: Putting It Together
objectives: Spot good AI use cases in your own work
Estimate value and risk of a use case
Plan a small, safe first project
keyPoints: Good first use cases are repetitive, text-heavy, low-risk, and easy to verify.
Estimate value by time saved or quality gained; estimate risk by impact if the AI is wrong.
Start small, measure results, and expand what works.
Skills compound: combine prompting, tool choice, and responsible use for real impact.
modules.example: Drafting first-pass replies to common customer questions is a great starter: high volume, easy to review, and low risk when a human approves each send.
modules.summary: Begin with repetitive, low-risk, easy-to-verify tasks, measure the benefit, and scale up responsibly. That is how AI literacy turns into real value.

questions.text: Which best describes today's artificial intelligence?
options: Narrow software that performs specific intelligent-seeming tasks
Conscious machines that understand like humans
A single program that can do anything
questions.explanation: Modern AI is narrow and task-specific, not conscious or general.

questions.text: Why does data quality matter so much in machine learning?
options: Models learn patterns from data, so biased or poor data yields poor models
Because more data always means perfect accuracy
Data quality only affects speed, not results
It does not matter once the model is trained
questions.explanation: Models generalise from their training data; flawed data produces flawed results.

questions.text: What does an LLM actually do to produce text?
options: Predicts the next token repeatedly based on patterns
Looks up answers in a fixed database
Copies sentences from the internet verbatim
questions.explanation: LLMs generate text by predicting likely next tokens.

questions.text: A 'hallucination' in generative AI is:
options: Confident output that is actually false
When the model refuses to answer
questions.explanation: Hallucination is plausible-sounding but incorrect output, so verification is needed.

questions.text: The 'context window' refers to:
options: How much text the model can consider at once
questions.explanation: It is the span of text the model attends to in one interaction.

questions.text: Which makes a prompt more effective?
options: Clear context, a specific task, and the desired format
questions.explanation: Context, task, and format guide the model to useful output.

questions.text: Before pasting information into an unapproved AI tool, you should:
options: Ensure no confidential or personal data is exposed
Paste everything to get the best answer
Assume privacy rules never apply online
questions.explanation: Data minimisation and policy compliance protect privacy.

questions.text: A responsible way to use an AI-generated answer is to:
options: Verify it against reliable sources before relying on it
questions.explanation: AI output should be checked before it is trusted.

questions.text: Which is the best first AI use case at work?
options: A task where errors are catastrophic
questions.explanation: Low-risk, verifiable, repetitive tasks are ideal starting points.

questions.text: For high-impact decisions like hiring or lending, AI should:
options: Support a human who remains accountable
questions.explanation: High-impact contexts require human oversight and accountability.

modules.title: What a Prompt Really Is
keyPoints: A prompt is the instruction and context you give an AI to steer its output.
Because the model predicts likely text, clearer prompts produce more relevant results.
Start from the outcome you want and work backwards into the instruction.
Vague prompts force the model to guess, which lowers quality.
modules.example: Prompt 'help with my email' is vague; 'rewrite this email to sound warmer and shorter, keep the meeting time' gives the model a clear target.
modules.summary: A prompt is how you steer an AI. The clearer your goal and wording, the better and more relevant the output.

modules.title: The Anatomy of a Strong Prompt
keyPoints: A strong prompt usually includes: context, a clear task, constraints, and desired format.
Assigning a role ('act as a financial analyst') focuses the response.
State constraints: length, audience, tone, and what to avoid.
Specify the output shape: bullet list, table, email, JSON, and so on.
modules.example: 'Act as a recruiter. Write 3 interview questions for a junior data analyst. Keep each under 20 words. Return as a numbered list.'
modules.summary: Structure prompts with context, task, constraints, and format. Adding a role and explicit limits dramatically improves results.

modules.title: Giving Context and Examples
objectives: Use examples to set a pattern (few-shot)
keyPoints: Context (audience, purpose, source text) grounds the answer in your situation.
Few-shot prompting means showing one or more examples of the desired input-output pattern.
Examples are powerful when you need a consistent style or format.
Too much irrelevant context can distract the model; include what matters.
modules.example: Show two sample product descriptions you like, then ask the model to write a third in the same style for a new product.
modules.summary: Relevant context and a few good examples teach the model your desired pattern, producing more consistent, on-target output.

modules.title: Controlling Format and Tone
keyPoints: Ask for the exact format you need: table, checklist, email, or a specific word count.
Name the tone (professional, friendly, persuasive) and the audience.
You can request machine-readable formats like JSON for use in other tools.
If the format is wrong, restate it explicitly and give an example.
modules.example: 'Summarise this report as a 5-row table with columns Risk, Likelihood, and Action. Professional tone.'
modules.summary: Tell the model exactly how the answer should look and sound. Explicit format and tone instructions remove guesswork.

modules.title: Iterating and Troubleshooting
keyPoints: Treat prompting as a conversation: refine with follow-ups instead of starting over.
If output is off, check for missing context, unclear task, or conflicting instructions.
Ask the model to critique or improve its own answer.
Save prompts that work well so you can reuse them.
modules.example: If a summary is too long, follow up with 'cut this to 80 words and keep only the top 3 points' rather than rewriting the whole prompt.
modules.summary: Good prompting is iterative. Refine with targeted follow-ups, diagnose what was missing, and reuse prompts that work.

modules.title: Prompting Safely and Responsibly
objectives: Recognise and reduce biased or false output
keyPoints: Do not paste confidential, personal, or regulated data into unapproved tools.
Verify facts and figures the model produces before using them.
Watch for biased framing and ask the model for balanced perspectives.
You remain responsible for anything you publish or act on.
modules.example: Before asking AI to draft a customer reply, remove personal account numbers and verify any policy details it includes.
modules.summary: Prompt responsibly: protect data, verify facts, check for bias, and remember that accountability for the output stays with you.

questions.text: A prompt is best described as:
options: The instruction and context that steer an AI's output
questions.explanation: A prompt is how you direct the model.

questions.text: Which elements make up a strong prompt?
options: Context, task, constraints, and desired format
As much unrelated detail as possible
questions.explanation: These four elements guide high-quality output.

questions.text: 'Few-shot' prompting means:
options: Showing examples of the desired input-output pattern
Using as few words as possible
Prompting only a few times per day
questions.explanation: Few-shot provides examples to set a pattern.

questions.text: To get a machine-readable result you might ask for:
questions.explanation: Requesting JSON yields structured, machine-readable output.

questions.text: If an answer is too long, the best next step is to:
options: Follow up asking to shorten it to a set length
Start a completely new chat each time
questions.explanation: Targeted follow-ups refine output efficiently.

questions.text: Assigning the model a role (e.g. 'act as an editor') tends to:
questions.explanation: Roles focus the style and substance of the answer.

questions.text: Before putting data into an unapproved AI tool you should:
questions.explanation: Protect sensitive data and follow policy.

questions.text: Who is responsible for AI output you publish?
questions.explanation: Accountability for published output stays with the user.

modules.title: AI in Everyday Life
objectives: Notice where AI already helps you
keyPoints: AI already powers maps, recommendations, photo tagging, voice assistants, and spam filters.
You do not need to code to use or understand AI.
AI works by spotting patterns in lots of examples.
Being curious and asking questions is the best way to learn it.
modules.example: When a streaming app suggests a show you end up loving, AI matched your viewing patterns to similar viewers.
modules.summary: AI is already woven into daily life through patterns learned from data. Anyone can understand and use it, no technical background required.

modules.title: The Big Ideas Behind AI
keyPoints: AI learns from examples rather than being told every rule.
It predicts likely answers; it does not truly understand meaning.
Training is the process of showing the system many examples until it spots patterns.
More and better examples usually mean better predictions.
modules.example: Show a child many pictures of cats and dogs and they learn to tell them apart. AI training works similarly with data.
modules.summary: At heart, AI learns patterns from examples and predicts likely outcomes. It is clever pattern-matching, not human understanding.

modules.title: What AI Can and Can't Do
keyPoints: Strengths: summarising, drafting, translating, answering common questions, spotting patterns.
Limits: it can be confidently wrong, lacks real-world judgement, and may be biased.
It does not know today's private facts about you or your company unless given them.
Always sanity-check important results.
modules.example: AI can draft a holiday itinerary in seconds, but it might list a museum that has closed, so you confirm details before booking.
modules.summary: AI is great for drafts, summaries, and patterns, but it can be wrong and lacks judgement. Trust it like a fast assistant whose work you check.

modules.title: Talking to AI Assistants
keyPoints: Say what you want, for whom, and in what form.
Add context the assistant would not otherwise know.
If the answer is not right, tell it what to change.
It is fine to ask it to explain things more simply.
modules.example: 'Explain compound interest to a 12-year-old in 3 short sentences' gets a clearer answer than 'explain compound interest'.
modules.summary: Clear questions with a little context get great results. Treat it as a conversation and refine until the answer fits.

modules.title: Staying Safe: Privacy, Bias, Misinformation
keyPoints: Avoid sharing sensitive personal details with public AI tools.
AI can reflect biases from its training data; question one-sided answers.
AI can produce convincing falsehoods, so verify before sharing.
Be cautious with AI-generated images, voices, and videos (deepfakes).
modules.example: Before resharing a striking AI-written 'fact', check a trusted source; it may be a hallucination.
modules.summary: Use AI safely by guarding your data, questioning bias, and verifying claims before you trust or share them.

modules.title: Bringing AI Into Your Day
keyPoints: Start with low-stakes tasks: drafting messages, summarising long text, brainstorming.
Build a habit by using it for one routine task this week.
Keep a note of prompts that work for you.
Skills grow with practice and curiosity.
modules.example: Use AI to turn a messy list of notes into a tidy summary email before a meeting.
modules.summary: Adopt AI gently with small, everyday tasks, build a habit, and keep experimenting. Confidence comes from practice.

questions.text: Which everyday tool commonly uses AI?
questions.explanation: Recommendations are a common everyday AI use.

questions.text: How does AI mainly learn?
options: By spotting patterns in many examples
By being told every rule by hand
questions.explanation: AI learns patterns from examples.

questions.text: A realistic limit of AI is that it:
questions.explanation: AI can produce confident but incorrect answers.

questions.text: To get a clearer answer from an assistant, you should:
options: Give context and say the format you want
questions.explanation: Context and format improve answers.

questions.text: A safe habit with public AI tools is to:
questions.explanation: Protect personal data with public tools.

questions.text: A 'deepfake' is:
options: AI-generated fake image, audio, or video
questions.explanation: Deepfakes are synthetic media that can mislead.

questions.text: Before sharing a surprising AI-written 'fact', you should:
options: Verify it with a trusted source
questions.explanation: Verification prevents spreading misinformation.

questions.text: A good first personal use of AI is:
options: A low-stakes task like summarising notes
questions.explanation: Start low-stakes and verifiable.

modules.title: Why AI Ethics Matters
objectives: Explain why ethics is central to AI
Frame ethics as risk management plus values
keyPoints: AI decisions can affect people's jobs, money, health, and rights at scale.
Ethical failures cause real harm, legal exposure, and loss of trust.
Stakeholders include users, the people decisions are made about, organisations, and society.
Ethics is both doing right by people and managing organisational risk.
modules.example: An automated benefits system that wrongly denies claims can harm thousands quickly, showing why ethics is not optional.
modules.summary: AI acts at scale, so ethical lapses cause broad harm and risk. Ethics protects people and organisations alike.

modules.title: Bias and Fairness
keyPoints: Bias often enters through unrepresentative or historically skewed training data.
A model can be accurate overall yet unfair to specific groups.
Mitigations: diverse data, fairness testing across groups, and human review.
Fairness has multiple definitions; choices should be explicit and documented.
modules.example: A hiring model trained on past hires may downgrade qualified candidates from under-represented groups, reproducing old bias.
modules.summary: Bias usually comes from the data and can make models unfair even when accurate. Test across groups and keep humans in the loop.

modules.title: Privacy and Data Protection
keyPoints: Collect and use the least personal data needed for the purpose.
Use data only for the purpose people consented to.
Regulations such as GDPR grant rights like access and deletion.
Anonymisation and access controls reduce privacy risk.
modules.example: Training a model on customer chats requires removing personal identifiers and confirming the use is permitted.
modules.summary: Protect privacy by minimising data, respecting consent and purpose, and meeting legal duties such as GDPR rights.

modules.title: Transparency and Accountability
keyPoints: People affected by AI should be able to understand and, where appropriate, contest decisions.
Disclose when AI is used in meaningful interactions or decisions.
A named human or body must remain accountable for outcomes.
Documentation (data, model, decisions) supports audits and trust.
modules.example: A bank using AI for loan scoring should explain key factors to applicants and let a human review disputes.
modules.summary: Be transparent about AI use and keep clear human accountability, supported by documentation people can rely on.

modules.title: Safety, Reliability and Human Oversight
keyPoints: Models can fail silently when conditions change, so monitor performance over time.
Higher-impact decisions need stronger human oversight (human-in-the-loop).
Test for misuse, edge cases, and adversarial inputs before deployment.
Have a fallback and an incident plan for when AI gets it wrong.
modules.example: A medical triage aid should flag uncertain cases to a clinician rather than deciding alone.
modules.summary: Ensure reliability through monitoring, oversight proportional to impact, and clear fallback plans for failures.

modules.title: Frameworks and Governance
keyPoints: The NIST AI RMF organises practice around Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage.
ISO/IEC 42001 defines an AI management system for organisations.
The EU AI Act takes a risk-based approach, with stricter rules for higher-risk uses.
Good governance starts with an inventory of where AI is used and its risk level.
modules.example: Classifying an AI use as 'high-risk' under the EU AI Act triggers extra obligations like risk assessment and documentation.
modules.summary: Frameworks like NIST AI RMF, ISO/IEC 42001, and the EU AI Act give structure to responsible AI. Governance begins with knowing where AI is used.

questions.text: Why is AI ethics especially important?
options: AI decisions can affect many people at scale
Ethics slows projects for no reason
questions.explanation: Scale magnifies both benefit and harm.

questions.text: Algorithmic bias most often originates from:
questions.explanation: Data is the most common source of bias.

questions.text: A core privacy practice is:
options: Collect only the data you actually need
questions.explanation: Data minimisation reduces privacy risk.

questions.text: Transparency in AI includes:
options: Disclosing AI use and enabling contestability
questions.explanation: People should know about and be able to challenge AI decisions.

questions.text: Human oversight should be strongest when:
questions.explanation: Oversight should scale with impact.

questions.text: The NIST AI RMF is organised around:
questions.explanation: These are the four NIST AI RMF functions.

questions.text: ISO/IEC 42001 defines:
options: An AI management system for organisations
questions.explanation: It is a management-system standard for AI.

questions.text: The EU AI Act primarily uses:
options: A risk-based approach with stricter rules for higher-risk uses
A total ban on all AI
questions.explanation: It tiers obligations by risk level.

modules.title: Foundations of AI in Supply Chain
objectives: Frame a use case around a decision
keyPoints: AI/ML learns patterns from data rather than applying only fixed rules.
Value comes from improving a specific decision, not from the technology itself.
Good candidates: forecasting, inventory, routing, supplier risk, and visibility.
Start where data exists and the decision is repeated often.
modules.example: Replacing a fixed reorder rule with a demand model that adapts to seasonality can cut both stockouts and excess stock.
modules.summary: AI in supply chain is about improving repeated decisions with data-driven models. Start where data and clear decisions already exist.

modules.title: Demand Forecasting & Planning with AI
keyPoints: MAPE measures average percentage error; bias shows consistent over- or under-forecasting.
Demand sensing uses near-real-time signals to adjust short-term forecasts.
A model can be accurate on average yet wrong for key items, so review at the SKU level.
Forecasts feed planning, so errors cascade into inventory and service.
modules.example: If a product is consistently under-forecast (negative bias), safety stock and reorder points may be set too low, risking stockouts.
modules.summary: Use metrics like MAPE and bias to judge forecasts, watch key items, and remember forecast errors ripple through the whole plan.

modules.title: Inventory Optimization
objectives: Explain safety stock and reorder point
keyPoints: Safety stock buffers variability; the reorder point triggers replenishment.
Reorder point covers expected demand during lead time plus safety stock.
Higher service levels cost more inventory; the trade-off must be explicit.
AI can set dynamic policies per SKU based on demand and lead-time variability.
modules.example: At 20 units/day with a 5-day lead time and 40 units of safety stock, the reorder point is 20x5 + 40 = 140 units.
modules.summary: Inventory policy balances service and cost. AI tailors safety stock and reorder points to each item's demand and lead-time variability.

modules.title: Logistics, Routing & Transportation
keyPoints: Optimizers minimise cost or distance subject to constraints.
Constraints include vehicle capacity, time windows, and driver hours.
AI can re-plan dynamically as orders and conditions change.
Outputs that break known constraints signal data or setup errors, not magic.
modules.example: A routing tool that proposes deliveries outside agreed time windows is flagging a data or configuration problem to investigate.
modules.summary: Routing AI optimises against real constraints. Treat constraint-violating output as a red flag to verify, not a result to trust blindly.

modules.title: Procurement & Supplier Intelligence
keyPoints: Good supplier risk scores combine financial, delivery, geographic, and compliance signals, and update over time.
Spend analytics reveals maverick spend and consolidation opportunities.
AI can monitor news and data for early supplier-risk warnings.
Decisions still need human judgement and supplier relationships.
modules.example: A rising risk score driven by a supplier's late deliveries and regional disruption prompts qualifying a second source early.
modules.summary: AI strengthens procurement with dynamic, multi-factor risk scoring and spend insight, while humans own the sourcing decisions.

modules.title: Warehouse Automation & Robotics
keyPoints: Computer vision supports scanning, sortation, and damage detection.
Robotics and AI assist picking, putaway, and movement.
Pilot a bounded, high-volume task first, then scale what proves out.
Measure throughput, accuracy, and safety before and after.
modules.example: Piloting vision-based sortation on one high-volume lane proves value and safety before rolling it across the warehouse.
modules.summary: Warehouse AI spans vision and robotics. Start with a bounded pilot, measure impact and safety, then scale deliberately.

modules.title: Risk, Resilience & End-to-End Visibility
objectives: Explain control towers and digital twins
keyPoints: A control tower gives real-time end-to-end visibility to detect and manage exceptions.
A digital twin simulates the network to test what-if scenarios safely.
AI can flag emerging disruptions earlier than manual monitoring.
Resilience means planning alternatives before disruptions hit.
modules.example: A digital twin can test how a port closure would ripple through deliveries, so contingency plans are ready in advance.
modules.summary: Visibility tools like control towers and digital twins, powered by AI, help detect disruption early and plan resilient responses.

modules.title: Responsible AI, Governance & Implementation
keyPoints: Keep humans in the loop on high-impact supply-chain decisions and watch for data bias.
Make the business case by linking AI to a decision and its cost or service impact.
Start small, measure, and scale; change management is as important as the model.
Align with governance frameworks such as the NIST AI RMF.
modules.example: A pilot that cuts forecast error by a few points and quantifies the inventory savings makes a fundable, defensible case.
modules.summary: Responsible adoption pairs human oversight and governance with a quantified business case and staged, measured rollout.

questions.text: What most distinguishes AI/ML from rules-based planning?
options: It learns patterns from data instead of only fixed rules
questions.explanation: ML generalises from data; rules apply fixed logic.

questions.text: MAPE measures:
options: Average percentage error of a forecast
questions.explanation: MAPE is a forecast-accuracy metric.

questions.text: The reorder point covers:
options: Expected demand during lead time plus safety stock
questions.explanation: Reorder point = lead-time demand + safety stock.

questions.text: Holding more safety stock:
options: Raises holding cost but lowers stockout risk
questions.explanation: It trades cost for protection against variability.

questions.text: In route optimization, constraints include:
options: Vehicle capacity, time windows, and driver hours
questions.explanation: Optimizers respect real operational limits.

questions.text: A strong supplier risk score:
options: Combines multiple signals and updates over time
Is set once and never changes
questions.explanation: Multi-factor, refreshed scoring is most useful.

questions.text: Before automating a warehouse process, the soundest step is:
options: Pilot a bounded, high-volume task and measure impact
questions.explanation: Bounded pilots prove value and safety first.

questions.text: A control tower provides:
options: Real-time end-to-end visibility to manage disruptions
questions.explanation: Control towers centralise visibility and exceptions.

questions.text: A digital twin is:
options: A virtual model to simulate and test scenarios
questions.explanation: Digital twins enable safe what-if testing.

questions.text: The strongest AI business case rests on:
options: A quantified link to a decision and its cost/service impact
questions.explanation: Funders back measurable decision improvements.
